name: Problem and market opportunity
summary: Creators and builders want to show work-in-progress without the storage cost and editing tax of full-frame screen recording. Timelapse-native tools compress hours of screen activity into short, shareable process videos by capturing at low effective frame rates or screenshot intervals, then encoding directly to sped-up output. The category is niche but durable: design speedpaints, coding sessions, UI builds, and course B-roll all map to the same job — document the process, not the meeting.
key_findings: Full-speed recording can reach ~7 GB/hour at high quality per TimeLapze marketing; Post-speed-up in editors adds 30–45+ minutes for polished tutorial workflows per Screen Studio ecosystem commentary; Hustl and Lapse position as zero-edit timelapse from menu bar; Setapp and BetaList list multiple timelapse SKUs proving ongoing demand; macOS ScreenCaptureKit (12.3+) lowered capture overhead vs legacy APIs
implications_for_trylapse: Anchor on reliability and instant export over feature breadth; Target makers who post process content to X/YouTube/Behance; Avoid competing on auto-zoom polish — that is a different category; Size opportunity as indie/SMB tool not enterprise
strengths: Clear job-to-be-done; Low ongoing cloud cost if local-first; Native macOS APIs mature enough for solo/small team
weaknesses: Niche TAM vs general screen recorders; Users may conflate with Screen Studio and expect editing; Hard to measure exact market size without paid incumbent revenue disclosure
metrics_or_signals: TimeLapze ~819 GitHub stars; OpenScreen ~37k stars (adjacent, not timelapse); Hustl on Setapp; Lapse on BetaList cross-platform

primary_sources.title: Screen Studio review — editing time tax
